critic_raw:
The claim that "temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split" is empirically false for hospital readmission data. Hospital readmission rates are driven by discharge planning protocols, follow-up care systems, and care coordinator availability — all of which evolve over time and exhibit strong within-institution temporal autocorrelation. A random split places observations from the same calendar period in training and test, allowing the model to learn period-specific practice patterns and apply them to held-out observations from the same period. This overstates generalization.

The RNN uses "time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, and follow-up visits" while the logistic regression uses "aggregate demographic and clinical variables." This is not an architecture comparison — it is a feature comparison. The logistic regression cannot exploit temporal structure because it is not given access to temporal features. If a logistic regression were given the same sequence summary statistics (visit frequency, decay-weighted recency, diagnosis code distribution over time), it would likely close much of the performance gap. The experiment design makes the RNN win by construction by giving it better inputs.

Recall as the primary metric creates a degenerate optimization target (predicting all patients as high-risk achieves 100% recall). The design partially acknowledges this by including precision and F1 as secondary metrics, but model selection is on recall, meaning the selected model may be one that calls almost everyone positive. Reporting that the RNN achieves higher recall than logistic regression tells the reader nothing clinically actionable without specifying the recall-precision operating point.

Patient-level leakage is not addressed. Patients with multiple admissions over three years will appear across train, validation, and test sets. The model learns individual patient patterns from earlier admissions (in training) and applies them to later admissions (in test). This is individual-level data leakage that does not reflect prospective deployment where a patient's prior history was genuinely unseen at the time of their first admission.

The design includes follow-up visits as features in the RNN sequence, but follow-up visits scheduled post-discharge may contain information about discharge planning decisions (high-risk patients are scheduled for more follow-up). If follow-up visit records from after the discharge date are included in the feature sequence, this is post-event leakage — the label (readmission) is predicted using features that are causally downstream of the discharge decision that determines readmission risk.
defender_raw:
The RNN readmission prediction design is methodologically sound in its core framing and correctly identifies the key design choices that make the comparison meaningful.

The RNN architecture is appropriate for time-stamped EHR sequence prediction. Time-stamped diagnosis codes, treatment histories, and follow-up visits encode a temporal trajectory of patient health that aggregate demographic and clinical variables cannot fully capture. The sequential dependence between visit events — where a follow-up visit for the same condition within days of discharge is a strong readmission risk signal — is exactly what RNN architectures model.

Recall as the primary metric is correctly motivated for this clinical context. The design explicitly justifies this choice by stakeholder emphasis on minimizing false negatives (missed high-risk patients). In readmission prediction used for care transition programs, a missed high-risk patient may not receive timely intervention, resulting in a preventable readmission. The direction of asymmetric cost is correctly identified.

The same limitation applies here as in eval_scenario_791: recall without a specified operating threshold is susceptible to the degenerate optimum of predicting readmission for all patients. However, the design reports AUROC, precision, F1, and recall as a suite, which mitigates this by providing threshold-independent metrics alongside the primary.

Preprocessing discipline — fitting normalization on training set only — is correctly specified and consistently applied to validation and test sets.

The logistic regression baseline uses 'aggregate demographic and clinical variables' with grid search for hyperparameter optimization. This is a fair comparison: it represents the traditional approach to readmission prediction that the sequence model aims to surpass.

The stratified random split with 100,000 patient records over three years is defensible at scale. The 'temporal autocorrelation is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split' justification is an empirical claim — whether it holds depends on whether the same patients appear across training and test with their later-period visits providing signal about earlier-period patterns.
all_issues_raised: The temporal stationarity assumption justifying random split is empirically unsupported; hospital readmission rates exhibit institution-level temporal autocorrelation driven by protocol evolution.

RNN receives sequential time-stamped features while logistic regression receives only aggregated demographics — the comparison measures feature richness, not model architecture; the RNN wins by construction.
Recall-optimized model selection selects the most aggressive positive predictor, producing a clinically useless model without specifying the recall-precision operating point.
Patient-level leakage: multiple admissions from the same patient across splits allow the model to exploit individual longitudinal patterns that would be unavailable in prospective deployment.

Follow-up visit records post-discharge may constitute post-event leakage if they encode discharge planning decisions that are causally downstream of readmission risk — these features must be excluded or carefully temporally bounded.
all_issues_adjudicated: RNN receives sequential time-stamped features while logistic regression receives only aggregated demographics — the comparison measures feature richness, not model architecture; the RNN wins by construction.
Recall-optimized model selection selects the most aggressive positive predictor, producing a clinically useless model without specifying the recall-precision operating point.
Patient-level leakage: multiple admissions from the same patient across splits allow the model to exploit individual longitudinal patterns that would be unavailable in prospective deployment.

Follow-up visit records post-discharge may constitute post-event leakage if they encode discharge planning decisions that are causally downstream of readmission risk — these features must be excluded or carefully temporally bounded.
